title: Wooden Plate Rack
summary: A wall-mounted plate rack from two 1x4s and two 1x2s — vertical slots hold plates on display and let them drip-dry. A one-board-bundle build.
description:
A plate rack is a classic farmhouse-kitchen fixture: plates stand on edge in slots, on display and draining. This one is deliberately minimal — two 1x4s for the sides, three for the shelves, and a set of rails and back supports — because once it's screwed to the wall, the wall provides most of the structure, so it doesn't need heavy joinery.

That's the key insight the plan leans on: you can build the rack with just glue and nails because the finished piece gets lag-anchored to wall studs, which stiffens the whole assembly. Always glue when you nail, but you don't need to over-build the box itself.

The rails are what actually hold the plates upright, spaced so a plate's rim rests against them without tipping out. Space them to the plates you own — dinner plates and salad plates want slightly different gaps. Hang it into studs through the back 1x2s under the shelves with 3" wood screws (on a standard stud-framed drywall wall); that back cleat is the load path, so it has to catch framing, not just drywall.

materials.name: Wood screws, 3"
materials.note: For hanging into studs — size to your wall.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut two sides at 32", three shelves at 28-1/2" and five rails and back supports at 28-1/2" from the 1x4. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to four 8 ft 1x4. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

steps.title: Build the rack body
steps.body: Attach the 1x4 sides and shelves to create the box. Nails are fine here since the finished rack gets anchored to the wall for extra structure — just always add glue when you nail. Keep it square.

steps.title: Add the rails and hang
steps.body: Glue and nail the rails and back supports to the framing to complete the rack, spacing the rails so your plates rest on edge without tipping out. Hang it on the wall into studs through the back 1x2s under the shelves with 3" screws — that cleat is the load path, so it must catch framing. Level it, sand, and finish.

images.alt: Wall-mounted wooden plate rack holding plates on edge
